data.title: 2 High-Yielding Picks For Retirees To Hedge Against Chaos
data.description:
If I had to summarize the start of 2026 in one word, I would go with “chaos.”

It is even difficult to decide where to start from. We have Venezuela, a credit card interest rate cap, an investigation against Fed Chair Jerome Powell, unprecedented protests in Iran, Greenland putting at risk the NATO alliance, and so on. All of this has basically happened in just 2 weeks time. We have not even reached the end of the first month in 2026.

At the same time, the bull market across the major indices (QQQ) (SPY) shows no signs of stopping. We are very close to peak valuations, the fourth year in a strong bull market, and despite the aforementioned turbulences, the markets just keep going up.

For me, it just makes it more and more difficult to justify holding pure-play exposures to the richly valued AI/tech names such as Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META). Everything that is in the top quartile of major AI-related indices makes me uncomfortable (and by saying this, I fully recognize that this kind of thinking has led to nowhere but a significant opportunity cost in the past couple of years).

The key risk that I see is that we really need just one major catalyst that would de-active the current “risk-on” mode to trigger huge outflows from the system. And as we know, the higher you climb, the harder you fall.

Apart from that, and irrespective of whether a more major selloff happens or not, there is already quite a risky process happening in the background—i.e., depreciation of the dollar.

The Fed's independence, increasing fiscal stimulus, and deep structural deficits all support the dollar losing its value (buying power). And it is not only an issue for international investors but also for U.S. market participants. The main consequence emerges in the form of increased (imported) inflation, making imported goods and services more expensive for U.S. consumers simply due to the unfavorable FX. The second-order effect of this is related to the interest rate curve, where stronger long-term inflation expectations tend to push up the yield levels across the curve, mostly in 2+ year departments (the past couple of quarters clearly confirm that yield-steepening is a risk).

In a nutshell, we are exposed to the following risks:

  • Weaker dollar.
  • Higher inflation.
  • Unexpected pop in the AI bubble.

I have identified two high-yielding assets that are well-positioned to ride this storm safely, offering investors the chance to even make some extra profits out of it.

#1 - NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI), 12%

The NEOS Gold High Income ETF is an out-of-the-money covered call ETF, which is dynamically managed, giving the management certain flexibility to decide when and how much to increase or reduce the option overwrite exposure.

As for most OTM covered call ETFs, the risk and return profile is quite different from common stocks, where there is, theoretically, unlimited upside and a maximum downside of 100%.

In IAUI's case, the upside is capped by the sold option strike values, and the downside is almost the same but slightly offset by the pocketed option premiums (i.e., not a full 100% loss).

So, if the underlying surges, the chances are extremely high that OTM covered call investors will face underperformance. If the underlying plunges, the OTM covered call investors will most probably experience similar pain.

It does not sound like a good deal.

Yet, we have to consider these two structural advantages:

  1. Covered call ETF income streams increase as the volatility of the underlying index goes up.
  2. The best-case scenario for these products is a gradual decline, increase, or sideways trading market that is punctuated with notable and short-term volatility spikes (e.g., a 3% drop, followed by a quick recovery).

Now, heightened volatility, not necessarily booming asset prices, but, at the same time, rather limited odds of sudden structural decline create a perfect setting for covered call vehicles and their income streams.

And if we speak about IAUI, there is another significant bonus that comes with deploying capital right into this ETF. It is called gold (GLD), which has in the past 3-year period appreciated by almost 140%.

In the context of higher long-term inflation, dollar depreciation, deep fiscal deficits, and increased geopolitical risks, gold is what can provide a store of value feature when the “fiat value” side looks increasingly stretched and under clear pressure.

Granted, gold has had a wonderful ride so far, which would raise a concern about a well-needed correction. But then again, we can make the same argument for almost any asset class (maybe not long-term plain vanilla bonds). However, what we cannot say about any other asset class is the inherent defense against inflation and a deteriorating dollar.

Plus, since IAUI offers an exposure to gold in the form of a covered call overlay mechanism, it means that we can actually make more money (juicier dividends) from higher vol, where the all-time highs could support that. Namely, it gives investors a reason to tactically reprice gold, while the strategic tailwinds provide support for long-term pricing.

Based on the trailing three-month distributions, we arrive at an annualized yield of 12%, which is excellent for income-oriented investors, who seek both high passive inflows and a fundamental hedge against the dollar, inflation, and geopolitical risks.

#2 - abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI), 11.8%

abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund is an infrastructure-focused closed-end fund that brings certain value points to the table that fit almost perfectly with the prevailing backdrop that we have in relation to what I have called “chaos.”

The main essence of ASGI is that it provides an exposure to infrastructure assets, which have some positive overlaps with gold. Since infrastructure is a physical asset, oftentimes irreplaceable and system-critical with a resilient demand profile, the store of value element kicks in here quite organically.

For example, most infrastructure assets are underpinned by long-term agreements with bankable (investment grade or government) offtakers, where the cash flows tend to be tied to CPI escalators. Apart from the CPI factor, the underlying asset itself also appreciates in value over time. It is just like buying and then renting out a home. You get money from tenants, and then after some years, you, generally, can sell with profit.

Another beauty of infrastructure, and, especially, in the case of ASGI, is that it provides access to long-term tailwinds—e.g., demand for data centers and reliable baseload energy. For example, ASGI has ~36% of its asset base located in the utilities and energy area.

Furthermore, given the pressures on the dollar, the fact that ASGI invests in other FX areas comes in extremely handy, introducing another source of income:

Now, a couple of days ago I issued an article on a quite similar vehicle to ASGI—NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund (SRV). My conclusion there was that SRV's yield was not that sustainable in the form of how it was funded—i.e., huge bias towards ROC even adjusted for the MLP (AMLP) K-1 distribution aspect, which is accounted as return of capital.

However, if we look at ASGI, we have a more sustainable story even though there is almost no external leverage involved that could magnify the core yield (see excerpt from the annual report):

The Fund currently does not intend to borrow money or issue debt securities or preferred shares. The Fund is, however, permitted to borrow money or issue debt securities in an amount up to 33% of the value of the Fund's total assets, and issue preferred shares in an amount up to 50% of its total assets.

For example, in the past 3-month period, ASGI has not touched ROC at all to make the monthly distributions happen. All of the cash has stemmed from net interest income and realized gains from attractive infrastructure assets that are subject to the aforementioned tailwinds.

Finally, the thing that I think speaks volumes is that since going public, ASGI has a) managed to increase distributions (5-year CAGR of 49%) and b) preserved NAV and, actually, increased it a bit from $20.00 per share to $21.35 per share.

ASGI, as an almost 12% yielding monthly income producer that comes with several timely hedge elements, is definitely something that I would integrate into durable retirement income portfolios.

data.title: Franklin Core Plus Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Market sentiment improved across the third quarter of 2025 (Q3) due to a partial resolution of some of the outstanding trade policy concerns. At its September meeting, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) cut the fed funds rate by 25 basis points (bps) and signaled that more cuts were likely to come by the end of this year. This bolstered markets which viewed that the cut was addressing some of the weakening economic measures, such as the status of the US labor market. As per Bloomberg, fixed income spread markets performed well as corporate bond spreads reached multidecade lows by the end of the quarter. Other fixed income sectors, such as securitized, also posted positive returns. The benchmark 10-year US Treasury yield fell eight bps to end the quarter at 4.15%.
  • Contributors: Overweight investment-grade (IG) corporate bonds and agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS).
  • Detractors: Security selection in senior secured-floating rate bank loans and HY and IG corporate bonds.
  • Outlook: There is high uncertainty about future Fed policy, even between members, as reflected in a large distribution seen in the Fed’s scattered projections for the path of fed funds for the remainder or the year. In our view, this level of uncertainty has the potential to spur volatility in the UST market.

Performance Review

  • Sector allocations were a strong driver of overall performance for the quarter as spreads moved tighter across most sectors, leading to excess returns. Our out-of-index exposure to a number of sectors—including HY corporate bonds, floating rate bank loans, collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), and agency collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs)—helped returns for the period. Additionally, overweight allocations to IG corporate bonds, MBS, and emerging market debt contributed to results.
  • Security selection weighed on performance, led by selection in floating-rate bank loans and HY and IG corporate bonds.
  • Yield curve positioning hurt results primarily due to an underweight exposure to the 10-year area of the yield curve.

Outlook

  • In our view, the Fed is unlikely to cut interest rates soon. Despite recent signs of cooling inflation, Fed officials remain cautious due to ongoing tariff-related uncertainties. Companies are expected to raise prices as pre-tariff inventories diminish, leading to a likely rise in core goods inflation. Furthermore, average US tariff rates remain historically high despite a temporary easing in US-China trade tensions. We therefore expect potentially higher inflation and slower growth. With inflation still above target and risks skewed to the upside, we expect the Fed to maintain its pause. Amid still-resilient industrial production and retail sales, we believe the Fed is likely to stay on hold until there is clear evidence of labor market deterioration without a simultaneous rise in inflation.
  • The “One Big Beautiful Bill” spending package will further increase the US deficit over the next decade. Combined with factors such as uncertain trade policy (which has raised stagflationary concerns) and an expansionary fiscal policy (which implies greater debt issuance), we expect longer-term rates to continue to climb higher through the rest of the year.
  • For the corporate credit sectors, we maintain our emphasis on diligent security selection and individual company fundamentals. IG credit fundamentals have remained supportive. While we believe IG corporate bonds continue to offer relatively attractive yield, spread levels offer little cushion for further economic, market or geopolitical surprises. In our view, spreads are likely to remain in a trading range but, at current levels, are more likely to widen than tighten. In the lower-credit quality HY and floating-rate bank loan sectors, although we remain broadly constructive amid generally supportive technical conditions, we continue to favor quality companies amid persistent market bifurcation and idiosyncratic opportunities as we evaluate each company’s fundamentals and exposure to different possible policy alternatives.
  • For securitized sectors, our generally neutral stance remains largely unchanged. Prepayment risk remains minimal, and mortgage credit quality remains high. The fundamental and technical outlook for the sector remains favorable, with attractive relative value to IG corporates. We also anticipate increased demand from banks if the yield curve normalizes or regulatory uncertainty dissipates. For RMBS, heightened macroeconomic uncertainty due to the Trump administration’s fiscal and foreign policies and tense geopolitical climate could result in more periods of elevated rate volatility and “risk-off” episodes. We therefore prefer a defensive positioning at the top of capital stack in higher-carry-profile bonds with limited extension risk. Despite our generally cautious stance, we are finding value and identifying opportunities in higher-quality fixed income investments.
Morningstar Rating™

Overall Rating as of September 30, 2025

★★★★★

(5-Star) Advisor Class

★★★★★

(5-Star) Class A

As of 09/30/2025 the fund’s Class A and Advisor Class shares received a 5 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk-adjusted returns against 520, 468 and 344 Intermediate Core-Plus Bond funds over the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.

Fund Characteristics

Fund

Distribution Frequency

Monthly

30-Day SEC Yield (Advisor Class)—With Waiver

4.46%

30-Day SEC Yield (Advisor Class)—Without

Waiver

4.39%

Average annual total returns and fund expenses (%) - as of September 30, 2025

Benchmark(s)

Benchmark = Bloomberg US Aggregate Index

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 8/12/1999. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (a) for Advisor Class periods prior to 8/12/1999, a restated ﬁgure is used based on the fund’s Class A performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-speciﬁc operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the speciﬁc share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 08/31/2026 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modiﬁed or discontinued at any time without notice.

During periods of rising inflation, fund yields can vary significantly from month-to-month and may not be repeated.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of ﬁxed income securities falls. Changes in the credit rating of a bond, or in the credit rating or ﬁnancial strength of a bond’s issuer, insurer or guarantor, may affect the bond’s value. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. Floating-rate loans and debt securities are typically rated below investment grade and are subject to greater risk of default, which could result in loss of principal. Asset-backed, mortgage-backed or mortgage-related securities are subject to prepayment and extension risks. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magniﬁed in emerging markets. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identiﬁed or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A basis point (bp, or bps) is one one-hundredth of one percent (1/100% or 0.01%).

A collateralized loan obligation (CLO) is a single security backed by a pool of debt.

Residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) are a type of mortgage-backed debt obligation created from residential debt, such as mortgages, home-equity loans and subprime mortgages.

Stagflation is a seemingly contradictory condition described by slow economic growth and relatively high unemployment, or economic stagnation, which is at the same time accompanied by rising prices (i.e. inflation).

The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

Effective December 11, 2024, the Franklin Strategic Income Fund changed its name to the Franklin Core Plus Bond Fund and modified its principal investment strategies. Such a change can impact performance.

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Index is comprised of investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated government, corporate, and mortgage- and asset-backed issues having at least one year to maturity. Source: Bloomberg Indices.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

Morningstar Rating™: Source: Morningstar®, 09/30/2025. For each mutual fund and ETF with at least a 3-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ based on how a fund ranks on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure against other funds in the same category. This measure takes into account variations in a fund’s monthly performance, and does not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund’s Class A shares received a Morningstar Rating of 5, 5 and 4 and fund’s Advisor Class shares received a Morningstar Rating of 5, 5 and 4 star(s) for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Franklin Templeton provides this fund’s Morningstar Rating™ for Class A and Advisor Class shares only. Other share classes may have different Morningstar ratings.

Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

© Franklin Templeton. All rights reserved.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Armada Hoffler Properties: The Valuation Disconnect Persists
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH), I highlighted their focus on deleveraging and shifting towards more predictable rental income while still paying a solid and affordable dividend even after a cut.

With the stock nearly flat since then despite a solid earnings report and mixed signals from the macro environment, they are still trading at what I believe to be clearly undervalued levels, which is why I’m reiterating their Buy rating.

Internal Developments

AHH reported a solid Q3, beating both the market’s revenue and FFO estimates by quite a bit, while the AFFO reached $19.075 million during the quarter and ~$57.84 million during the first 9 months of 2025. Annualized, that’s roughly $77.12 million per year, which is certainly not bad for a ~$718.5 million market cap, placing them at a P/AFFO ratio of about 9.3.

However, note that that’s still a significant drop on a per share basis ($0.57 in 9M’25 vs $0.76 in 9M’24) as the number of shares increased significantly to fund the completion of their Southern Post mixed-use development and deleverage the balance sheet. The dilution doesn’t have to be bad in the long-term, while the company shifted from relying on volatile third-party construction fees to focus on more stable rental income.

AHH also narrowed their 2025 Normalized FFO outlook to $1.03 to $1.07 per share, helped by higher NOI that helped offset their construction adjustments, while also giving some guidance regarding Southern Post stabilization.

As a note, the overall weighted average (stabilized) occupancy rate was 95.7% back in Q3 2025, which is still solid in this market. Per branch, that’s 96% for retail, 96.5% for office and 94.2% for multifamily.

Financially, based on AHH’s latest report, we see an overall normal position for a REIT, with limited cash but significant assets that more than offset their debt.

We can see that their debt increased compared to 2024 especially following the July $115 million senior notes (inaugural private placement with maturities of 3, 5 and 7 years and a blended interest rate of 5.86%), but the management expects this ratio to drop naturally in 2026 as the EBITDA would increase thanks to property stabilization and they potentially sell non-core assets to pay down the debt. As the company’s CFO mentioned during the Q3 Earnings Call:

Capital markets remain selective, and we are structuring our balance sheet to reflect that reality. With our debt private placement completed in July and our liquidity stabilized through prudent cash management, we have the ability to remain patient and disciplined as the cycle evolves.

AHH currently trades at a strong dividend yield of ~8.14%, which is above their preferred (AHH.PR.A) that currently pays ~7.89%. The preferred stock is callable from June 2024, but it really wouldn’t make much sense to call them now when buybacks would theoretically achieve much more, as these are 6.75% yielding preferred shares based on their cost basis for the capital originally raised ($25 par value), so the risk-reward on them is very solid.

As they say, the AFFO payout ratio stands at 74.9%, and after taking out the non-cash interest income, we should look at about 93.9%. They also mentioned the following regarding buybacks:

I don't want to say we're going to get into buyback land, but certainly, there's an attractive accretive opportunity there. I'm not sure that we'll take that versus long-term property -- kind of income-producing property. But yes, that's what we are doing right now, especially given the price of the equity and how that's trading in today's market.

Macro Developments

Although rate cuts advanced at the end of 2025, we continued to see significant uncertainty in the macro environment, with pressure both from international factors (e.g. Venezuela, Greenland, Iran) and also domestic ones (e.g. Fed independence, consumer weakness, real estate policies, etc.), which can ultimately result in an even bigger delay between the Fed’s actions and the actual cost of external capital for the companies and people.

Ultimately, being a REIT limits the company’s ability to fund new initiatives (especially during a strategic shift) as they have to pay most of what they make back to us, relying on external capital (issuing stock, issuing debt, asset recycling) for funds. In this environment, it’s no wonder that REITs are underperforming, as on top of occupancy/tenant issues, they also have to face significantly higher costs.

As we can see in AHH’s case, issuing stock is nearly out of the question since it would cost about 8% (the dividend - unless they cut it; would cost even more if they increase it in time) and they are actually considering the opposite (buybacks), while the most recent debt issuance came with a 5.86% weighted average interest, which makes the ~7% or so interest they can generally get on their investments really low for this cost.

This was a completely different story before the pandemic, when issuing stock cost about 4% to 5% and the SOFR was much lower.

Especially following a 9-3 vote in favor of the December cut (biggest disagreement since 2019) based on the December minutes as some participants see it fitting to pause now, combined with the DOJ criminal investigation into Powell and rising fear around the Fed’s independence after Powell’s term ends in May 2026, I believe there is risk of seeing capital costs at least remaining elevated for longer - if not even increase.

This would technically be the market’s way of saying it wants a better yield for the new political/economic/etc. risk it would take when lending to the US, potentially leading to more international diversification as the US could risk eroding its safe haven status.

However, looking at it from a longer-term perspective, if we see an improving consumer/business environment combined with the general fall in new supply across all of AHH’s real estate segments, there is certainly room for a recovery in both broader economics and the market’s opinion/demand for AHH and similar high-yield stocks.

This fall in new supply comes after a post-pandemic boom in many segments of real estate, while the market is now facing rising costs of building new properties (higher construction, workforce, debt, etc. costs) combined with weak occupancy and uncertainty, which can turn this around.

Risks Reiterated

Although a broader market recovery is possible, the company still faces high capital costs in an overall weak environment, with potential for the environment to get worse before any potential positive impact from the supply side would be visible, which could also affect their potential asset sales.

Especially as a big chunk of their long-term debt is due over the next couple of years, any near-term issue in the environment could have negative implications for years ahead, hurting the company even long after these issues are over.

Valuation

Starting with AHH’s annualized AFFO of $77.12 million in 2025, I’ll assume a 6% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to their project pipeline, debt repayments and potential for lower interest expenses, with a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as an 10% discount rate and 102.09 million shares outstanding as of their Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $1.18 billion and an intrinsic value of $11.55 per share, which hints at a very strong margin of safety compared to the current $6.88, being well above this level even if we use much more conservative estimates.

Conclusions

AHH remains a Buy, as the stock continues to be undervalued in an environment with strong potential to improve while the company is delivering solid results.

There is obviously risk as we’re talking about a REIT, which exposes them significantly to external capital costs, but the overall risk-reward is attractive from these levels, while the dividend yield and potential buybacks on top are also offering a very nice boost to the investment case.

data.title: The Great Rotation: Money Flows Out Of Big Tech And Into Small Caps
data.description:
If you got lost in what were largely meaningless headlines yesterday, you missed what was going on beneath the surface—rotation. Stocks opened lower after bank earnings failed to impress investors. We had delayed reports on retail sales and the Producer Price Index for November, which are so dated that they are not even worth commenting on. Hopes for a Supreme Court ruling on the legality of tariffs were dashed by late morning, with the next possible date being Wednesday. Of course, we had another barrage of political and geopolitical headlines, but few are relevant to what is now the most overarching investment theme in markets today.

As I asserted would happen at the beginning of this year, investors continue to rotate out of the expensive technology sector with a decelerating rate of earnings growth and into smaller companies that have more reasonable valuations and greater increases in earnings growth. While the S&P 500 has gained 1.2% so far this year, and the Nasdaq Composite is less than 1% higher, the Russell 2000 small-cap index has soared nearly 7%. The equally weighted S&P 500, which best represents the average stock, has gained 3.7%, while the Magnificent 7 (MAGS) are collectively down 1.4%. Stocks left behind during the first three years of this bull market are moving to the front of the pack.

There is nothing unusual about this, as stocks respond to rates of change. As I outlined in my 2026 Outlook, the rate of earnings growth for the Magnificent 7 decelerated last year to approximately 22% from more than 37% the year before. It increased from 5% to 9% for the remaining 493 names.

The rally in small caps started during the second half of last year, as investors anticipated a far more robust rate of change in earnings for the Russell 2000 index this year. It is as simple as that.

This strength also reflects confidence in more robust economic growth from monetary and fiscal stimulus, and we are starting to see sectors of the economy that have been stagnant come back to life. One that stands out is housing, which I wrote about last week as being a low-risk opportunity in advance of several efforts by the Trump administration to stimulate demand. Yesterday, we saw some of the first signs of life. Existing home sales rose in December at the fastest pace since 2023. Sales rose 5.1% over the previous month to an annualized rate of 4.35 million, which is the highest figure since February 2023. Additionally, the median sale price of an existing home sold rose just 0.4% from a year ago to $405,000. That is disinflation at work.

New home sales declined 0.1% in October over the prior month to an annualized rate of 737,000, but that is 18.7% above the year-ago level. We are finally above pre-pandemic levels for new home sales. The median price of a new home sold was $392k, which is down 8% from the prior year, while the average price was $498k, which is down 4.6% from the prior year. Some of this has to do with homebuilders offering incentives to move inventory, but that is not a bad thing for consumers.

Affordability is also improving as incomes are now rising at a faster rate than existing home prices, and the cost for a 30-year fixed-rate mortgage is gradually approaching 6%. The National Association of Realtors’ affordability index, which tracks the average family’s ability to qualify for a loan, is now at its highest point in nearly three years.

The housing market may not come roaring back, but I see gradual progress throughout 2026, which bodes well for a sector of the economy that has been stagnant for several years. I also see tremendous upside in companies benefiting from the recovery, especially those in the business of mortgage origination, servicing, and search, as well as retailers focused on home furnishings, appliances, renovation, and repair. There might be a few small caps in there too!

data.title: 2 REITs That Pay Me $1,000 Each Month
data.description:
One of the main reasons why I invest so heavily in real estate is that it provides high and consistent income.

With regular stocks, I feel like I'm completely at the mercy of the market, and I don't like it. It feels much more speculative as my returns are entirely dependent on whether other investors are willing to bid up the share price or not. It works well when the market is rising, like in recent years, but as we go through times of declining or stagnating prices, you may be left with nothing for years.

With real estate, I'm at least getting paid generously to wait. Each property has tenants, typically tied to a long-term lease, that pay you monthly to use the property. This helps a lot to stay patient as I earn a decent return regardless of what the market does next.

Today, we're going to look at two of my biggest monthly dividend-paying REITs (VNQ) that pay me a total of $1,000 each month.

Over time, I expect them to grow in value as well, but even if they don't, I'm more than happy to simply collect my growing passive monthly income.

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / BSRTF)

We start with the lowest yielder.

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / BSRTF) is today offering a 4.6% dividend yield paid on a monthly basis. That may not seem high, but keep in mind that the REIT retains about 40% of its cash flow to reinvest in growth, which has allowed it to grow its dividend at a rapid pace in recent years.

This REIT focuses on apartment communities in the rapidly growing Texas Triangle of Dallas, Austin, and Houston:

These markets are experiencing some of the fastest population and job growth in the entire nation:

Even then, BSR's average rent is only about $1,500 per month, which represents a low 20% rent-to-income ratio on average.

I think that as these markets keep growing at a rapid rate, they will continue to see their rents rise to higher levels over time.

But this, of course, isn't a perfectly steady process. In recent years, these markets became oversupplied as too many developers thought to capitalize on this opportunity and launched projects back when interest rates were ultra-low. This led to stagnating rents and occupancy rates in 2024 and 2025.

But now conditions are again improving. The new supply under construction has dropped to a decade-low level and continues to decline further each month that passes. Developers are finishing what they have underway, but very few new projects are breaking ground because it has become very difficult, if even possible, to build new communities at a discount to replacement cost.

Put simply, the high inflation in construction costs and the higher interest rates have made most new development projects economically unfeasible, and that's especially true at the rent level at which BSR is leasing its properties.

This means that we're likely to see little new supply over the next few years, even as these markets keep attracting a lot more people, which should ultimately lead to solid rent growth:

Despite that, BSR is today priced at an exceptionally low valuation. The recent oversupply, coupled with the surge in interest rates, has caused its growth to stagnate and hurt its market sentiment. As a result, it now trades at a 30% discount to its net asset value, which is truly exceptional for a REIT with such attractive long-term prospects.

The management is taking advantage of this low valuation to buy back shares hand over fist. This should create a lot of value for shareholders over time, and as we go into the apartment bull market, I expect the valuation discount to close, potentially resulting in 40% upside from here.

While we patiently wait for the upside, we earn a steady 4.6% dividend yield, paid monthly, and that still leaves significant cash flow for the REIT to keep reinvesting in growth.

That's far more attractive than what you could achieve on your own by buying a private apartment community, in my opinion: Higher yield, lower valuation, completely passive, diversified, and liquid.

Hard to beat that.

Canadian Net REIT (NET.UN:CA)

Now we move to a higher yielder.

Canadian Net REIT (NET.UN:CA) is my single largest monthly dividend-paying REIT investment.

It is currently offering a 6.5% dividend yield, paid monthly. Just like BSR, its dividend is very well covered with a low 55% payout ratio, which leaves significant cash flow for the REIT to keep reinvesting in growth.

So far this year, it has grown its cash flow by 9% and also hiked its dividend by 1.5%, and that's just the tip of the iceberg.

The REIT has one of the best growth track records of any REIT. Just look at how fast it has managed to grow its cash flow over the long run:

How has it achieved this?

I have previously described the REIT as an early-day Canadian version of Realty Income (O) in that it's much smaller, operates with more leverage, and faces less competition for acquisition, and this allowed it to earn higher spreads and grow at a faster pace.

It's the only REIT focusing on net lease properties in Canada, targeting primarily service-oriented properties such as grocery stores, quick-service restaurants, pharmacies, and gas stations, which it then leases on a long-term basis under triple-net leases.

They generate highly consistent rental income as their leases have many years left on them, and rents are pre-agreed for the entire duration of the lease, with automatic annual rent escalations. The tenants are also typically responsible for all property expenses, including even the maintenance of the property.

And the beauty of operating in Canada is that their interest rates are already a lot lower at just 2.25% at the short end of the curve, yet their cap rates are just as high, if not higher, than in the US because there's a relative lack of buyers.

This allows Canadian Net REIT to consistently earn superior spreads, which results in faster FFO per share growth, and given that it's still very small in size with its $300 million portfolio, we think that it should be able to keep this going for a long time to come.

Despite growing at a rapid pace, the REIT is priced at a low valuation of just 8x FFO and a 6.5% dividend yield that's safely covered and paid monthly.

I think that as interest rates continue to decline and yield-starved investors return to the REIT sector, this REIT will regain popularity and is likely to reprice at a materially higher valuation.

This is why I have heavily invested in this REIT, and it now generates more passive monthly income than any other monthly-paying stocks in our portfolio at High Yield Landlord.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Price Reset Creates A Compelling Total Return Opportunity
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital (OWL) merges two strong theses that often attract income investors' attention: resilient private credit income combined with AI infrastructure-led demand. My thesis supports OWL's positioning as a decent way to earn yields while earning from long-term secular growth.

OWL's credit discipline holds good so far, and the data center exposure seems to reduce default risks. I also see better payout coverage ahead, driven by earnings power improvements. For a stock where price fluctuations are the primary driver of total returns, the current yields, when combined with the risk-reward asymmetry, make for a compelling total return bet. The business quality remains good, and the support from data centers should help a Buy rating. Speculative total return investors can consider a small position, anticipating execution to eventually rerate the stock even higher.

Income and Embedded Expectations

The Fee Related Earnings (FRE) reported in Q3 2025 were $0.24 per share. Distributable Earnings (DE) was $0.22 per share. The quarterly payout of $0.225 therefore translates to a payout ratio of ~102%. This is not a hugely underfunded payout but shows that OWL relies on future growth to a large extent to normalize the payout ratio over time and even add some conservative buffer, ideally. So, my expectations on dividend growth are subdued for the near term. The dividends are stretched now, and the best-case scenario is that they are maintained and better funded.

The share price is now trading at ~16x FRE (annualizing $0.24 FRE per share reported in Q3). This is definitely not distressed valuations. I collated quarterly FRE data since 2022-23 and used quarter-end share prices as an indication of FRE multiple-based valuations (indicative because OWL share prices fluctuate materially intra-quarter). The valuation trends show an expected compression from the rate-supported highs. They are comforting in the sense that they do seem to respect the rate normalization regime underway and do not seem to have been unusually blown up due to the AI narrative that has emerged in the past couple of years.

The FRE and DE growth trends over the past year also look supportive and explain why markets are still assigning healthy valuations in an otherwise mid-cycle macro environment. Management has also indicated meaningful acceleration in FRE and DE numbers through 2027. So, OWL's current payout ratio is definitely not looking threatened.

Another way to look at valuations is the yield itself. A close to 30% correction in share prices in the past year has mechanically boosted yields. This shows that share prices have indeed retracted to a level that reflects the macro conditions. These yields are now more meaningful on a cost basis, and as long as the more dominant total return driver—share prices—can prove a favorable risk-reward asymmetry, the yields become a supporting factor for the Buy thesis. I will pause here and remind readers that I look at OWL as a total return vehicle (given its price volatility)—not a pure income vehicle alone.

Optionality and Favorable Risk-Reward

There are some already identified earnings drivers that can be tapped into. This could support upside in share prices even without valuation rerating. Management highlighted ~$28b of AUM not yet paying fees and expects ~$360m in management fees from these assets.

I also see the AI infrastructure exposure as a stabilizing influence in OWL's portfolio, if not a margin growth driver. What is opening up now for OWL now is long-dated contracted revenues with investment-grade counterparties and the potential to add volume and scale, if not margins. The economics are also protected from early termination. As per management commentary in Q3 2025,

If there were an early termination, there is a perfectly mathematical make whole where we make -- the debt makes all its money.

Even if AI exposure may not immediately mean improved unit economics, it expands the fee-paying AUM over time, which supports FRE growth. This raises the floor on credit stress outcomes due to protected long-dated contracts with high-quality tenants.

Risks to the Thesis

The biggest risk to the Buy thesis for OWL is execution misses. The deployment of expected fees from AUMs not paying fees currently may reduce odds of FRE growth and share price upside, even if the floor may still be well defended at current valuations.

I will also watch payout ratios over the next 2-3 quarters to see if stretched payouts are still underway. Any dividend cut in itself may not be concerning for the Buy thesis, as it relies on total returns. However, income appeal could trigger further compression in valuation.

The usual credit deterioration risks due to stress in broader credit markets also exist but do not seem material today. Average annualized losses are still looking reasonable -

As we have highlighted in previous earnings calls and continue to call out, the health of our credit portfolio remains excellent with an average annual realized loss of just 13 basis points and no signs of meaningful stress. In direct lending, the modest level of nonaccruals we have seen are not thematic in nature, and there's not been an uptick in our watch list levels.

Good Entry Point

Finally, a look at historical 3-year total return regimes shows the 3-year rolling returns have never been negative, and the current 3-year total return of ~45% lies at the lower end of the spectrum. This reinforces the fact that this is not an overheated regime fresh entries today will walk into. A local low in 3-year returns also implies either a deteriorating business model, which is not the case looking at the AI contracts and growth in FRE. Therefore, this reset is more about valuations and sentiment while the earnings durability outlook remains intact.

A lower 3-year total return is not necessarily a timing tool that signals reasonable forward returns, but it increases the odds of favorable outcomes over the next 3 years. And supports the bull thesis based on fundamentally sound pillars like FRE growth, stable credit performance, and improving earnings visibility.

data.title: From K-Shaped To J-Shaped: A 10% Credit Card Cap Has A Winner
data.description:
Introduction

Things are moving really fast this year. We have almost forgotten about Venezuela, even though it happened less than two weeks ago. After all, almost every day we have seen breaking news that has shaken some sectors in the market, with Greenland, Iran, and Jerome Powell catching most of the attention.

However, a small parenthesis of the daily debate was filled by the news that President Trump wants a 10% cap on credit card rates for one year, a measure that could be effective as early as January 20. The reaction was immediate as credit card stocks as well as credit card issuers fell sharply.

To say it all, this proposal should not have been a surprise, since back in February 2025, Senator Sanders introduced the "10 Percent Credit Card Interest Rate Cap Act", which is currently being discussed. However, President Trump's words placed this bill at the center of the domestic political discussion, up to the point that several banks had to warn that this choice would limit access to credit.

Here, I would like to discuss a few aspects that I have not seen truly considered. First and foremost, in the case the bill is approved, we will have to see whether it will be applied only for one year, as the President said, or until January 2031, as it is written in the bill's text.

Since it is because of the President's statement that we are considering the cap rate, I will mainly focus on the consequences of a cap for one year.

The Problem

The 10% cap appears as a solution to a problem that "The Consumer Credit Card Market" report, just issued at the end of December 2025, clearly highlights: the cost of credit, measured through average percentage rate (APR) levels, is at decade-highs. The report highlights that while cardholders increased 9% from 2022 to 2024, the overall amount of interest charges increased by 52%, which created an extra $45B in charges driven by higher rates and not by strong growth in the cardholder base.

Now look at the chart below. The orange line shows the commercial bank interest rate on credit card plans at the end of 2025 (the report I mentioned above stops at the end of 2025), where we see that the current APR is 21.4% (and this is an average; there are credit cards that charge up to 30%). At the same time, the purple line shows that credit cards and revolving plans have been rapidly increasing after the pandemic. When we put these two lines together, we see that while rates were skyrocketing, credit card balances also exploded. It is something we would not think based on what we know about macroeconomics: when rates go up, usually the consumer borrows less. It happened just the opposite; the consumer is borrowing more. Surely, this is linked to another fact that I talked about recently: extra savings from the pandemic have long run out, and the savings rate is currently very low. In other words, we are seeing loan demand driven by necessity. In other words, credit card loans are a non-discretionary item, and they play the role of an income substitute or strengthener.

This is the issue. American households are short on savings. It is not so simple, though. As we have recently seen in another article I wrote, credit card debt by income group is quite different: it is the middle class that is borrowing more than anyone else, while lower-income households are starting to reduce their debt after it peaked in early 2024, and high-income earners are actually carrying less debt now than they did before the pandemic struck.

Considering that a little over two-thirds of the U.S. GDP comes from consumption expenditures (see chart below), the fact that APRs are high implies that the consumer, especially the middle class, is now particularly pressured and strained even though, as JPMorgan's CEO just said during the last earnings call, "consumers have money", pointing out that employment is high and that the OBBBA provides meaningful stimulus.

Now, we better understand one of the elements that drives rates so high. While the Fed is actually lowering rates, banks have to price a premium for the risk of delinquencies, which is something that can't be simply ruled out, given the average balance sheet of American households.

This is not the place to teach or preach. But, surely, this situation shows why a pillar of healthy household finance should be simply to avoid credit card debt and learn to live a bit below one's means. This would also have the effect of having access to nice perks such as cashback programs.

The Effects of the "Solution"

Now, there is no doubt that if the cap were to be approved for a long period of time, we would probably see a shock in credit supply: card approvals would rapidly decrease, private label cards could be flushed down the toilet, and weaker borrowers would probably lose access to the credit market. The middle class, in particular, would be exposed to loan sharks or start turning to pawn shops, which usually apply "service fees" that represent real APRs of up to 500% (now, that's usury!). Delinquencies would increase (the New York Fed calculated that, as of Q3 2025, around 4.5% of outstanding debt is in one of the delinquency stages).

Let's tackle the easy part of this paragraph: credit card issuers. If rates are capped at 10%, the negative delta is 11.4 percentage points. Below, you see how revolving consumer credit increased; as of November 2025, it stood at $1.31T. This means that the gross interest revenue reduction for credit card issuers could be close to $150B a year. This is what's at stake, and it is a huge sum. Of course issuers would tighten their issuances and would lower credit card limits to reduce the risk. A price ceiling, therefore, would put pressure on risk premia, pushing them down. This would squeeze weaker borrowers out of the credit market. I would bet new marketing campaigns would target higher-income households, offering them any sort of deal. A waterbed effect could also take place: while credit card rates move down because of the cap, credit card issuers could raise prices elsewhere to compensate (an annual fee for just holding a card could be a solution, for example). Here, the scenario is clear: lower earnings for credit card issuers and fewer consumers participating in the money lending and borrowing market.

However, I think that the market is getting it wrong with network companies, namely Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA). I don't think these companies are directly vulnerable because they actually don't care a lot about APR. The real consequence could be that, if credit tightens, volumes could become softer. But, in reality, when we deconstruct how the APR cap would work, we see that it is meant to transfer profits away from the issuers to benefit the borrowers.

Now, here is where a short-term scenario kicks in. I said above that I wanted to deal with the one-year cap. If this is what the President wants to be approved by Congress, then things could change compared to the doomsday narrative that we are hearing.

First of all, if people do believe that the cap will only last one year, then I don't think card issuance could be severely hurt. Issuers would perhaps be a bit more cautious, but they would also benefit from the fact that, after one year, they would be able to earn higher interest on a higher amount of debt. We have to be realistic: if the APR is lower, I don't see it likely for households to suddenly change their spending habits and become strict observers of Dave Ramsey's hate for debt. I actually see the contrary happening: it will feel cheaper to borrow money. Which means that credit card issuers are simply postponing their high-margin earnings to the next year. True, after a year there could be some sort of deal that caps rates at 15% or around that. A deal would have to be found eventually, but only if we consider a longer timeframe. A year goes by quite quickly.

This is the bridge argument that leads us to the consumer. Unfortunately, due to the last government shutdown, the latest economic release updates have been delayed, and we are still stuck with the data from last September, when personal income grew 0.4%. However, the Fed's last G.19 report on consumer credit shows that revolving credit decreased 1.9% YoY in November, which is awkward since, during the holiday season, these balances usually increase. It could be a sign that some households are pulling back on revolving credit because it has become truly too expensive.

If this is the correct interpretation, then it becomes easier to support the following argument: a one-year cap would probably fuel a spending increase due to the fact that borrowing suddenly becomes cheaper. A lower rate would also reduce the efforts of many consumers to pay down their debt, which would also contribute to an increase in overall credit card debt.

Technically, we could see a move from a K-shaped economy to a shallow J-curve, where we see a sudden improvement in spending, which would be followed by a slowdown as issuers adapt to the new cap by tightening underwriting and bringing down credit limits. But the second part of this curve could be avoided if the cap lasts just one year.

Do you see where this leads? What happens within one year? Midterm elections. I don't want to raise a political discussion, but I would like to highlight that this topic comes out in a year when how consumers feel about their finances and the overall economy matters more.

Finally, we should not underestimate what could happen inside the financial world during this year. I am in the field of those who believe that financial companies will be among the first beneficiaries of AI since they have huge datasets and can automate many internal tasks. One year with a cap would speed up the process toward AI that has already started, pushing banks and other credit card issuers to change their internal cost structure to be more competitive. As the year expires and rates can move higher, many of these companies could find themselves with a leaner cost structure, thus seeing that even a lower APR is sustainable and can lead to hefty margins. This might be too speculative, but I would not overlook the efficiency gains that many financial stocks will benefit from.

Conclusion

As a result of what we have discussed, I am not concerned by the 10% cap rate, and my overall moderately bullish look on the market is not changed. I have indeed bought more of Visa in recent days and will buy more of Mastercard in the next few, as I think investor fears around these two, especially around the latter, which has great cross-border exposure that would not be affected by the cap, are overblown.

data.title: TSMC: Set For New All-Time Highs
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) aka "TSMC" submitted better-than-expected earnings for its fourth fiscal quarter amid strong demand for AI-optimized chips. TSMC achieved record profitability in Q4 '25 due to robust demand for GPUs and AI accelerators, resulting in the fab reporting its seventh consecutive quarter of 10%+ year-over-year earnings growth. Further, shares are very reasonably valued, based off of earnings, and the chip enterprise is likely going to grow its revenues another ~30% this year as demand for AI-driven accelerated computing is expected to remain strong.

TSMC continues to control the fab/chip manufacturing market based off of Trendforce data which makes the enterprise a strategic long term investment for investors that have a favorable view on the Data Center market and accelerating adoption of AI technologies.

Previous rating

I rated shares of TSMC a Strong Buy -- Strong Buy At All-Time Highs -- in October 2025 due to the fab being able to expand its top line as well as its earnings in light of strong secular demand for new AI chips. Demand for new semiconductors that are optimized for artificial intelligence applications has been surging, and TSMC indicated with its Q1 '26 outlook that it expects robust demand to continue.

TSMC also beat its own gross profit margin outlook for Q4 '25 and with semiconductor sales likely to continue to surge in 2026, the foundry play will remain a top growth investment for investors, in my opinion. I expect TSMC to achieve new all-time highs in 2026 and I also believe that the firm's earnings scorecard for Q4 '25 sets other chip plays like Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) up for new all-time highs this year.

AI rally set to continue for TSMC in 2026

In the fourth fiscal quarter TSMC generated better-than-expected revenue and earnings due to a highly beneficial demand situation in the high-performance computing segment: the Taiwan-based chip giant generated $3.14 in normalized EPS, beating the consensus estimate by $0.16. In the terms of revenue, TSMC generated a top line of $33.7B which out-matched the consensus estimate by $1.0B.

TSMC achieved strong revenue growth in the fourth-quarter, due to the company serving the largest chip design companies, including Nvidia, AMD, and Intel (INTC) with chip manufacturing services. In FY 2025, the company generated a total T$3.8 trillion in revenue, showing 31.6% year-over-year growth... which the chip enterprise already revealed in last week's preliminary results. In the most recent quarter, Q4 '25, TSMC saw 25.5% year-over-year growth (in USD terms) and posted total revenue of $33.7B.

TSMC benefited from red-hot demand for its 3nm, 5nm and 7nm semiconductors that are used in Data Centers in order to power AI applications. The firm's outlook, discussed further below in the valuation section, indicates that the chip company expects this momentum to last, driven by the HPC segment.

In the fourth quarter, the chip maker generated a total gross margin of 62.3%, showing 3.3 PP growth compared to the year-earlier period. Further, the fab easily exceeded its own margin guidance which called for a gross profit margin of 59-61% in Q4 '25. In the previous quarter, TSMC's gross profit margin was 59.5%, so the company squeezed out a nice 2.8 PP margin gain here as well.

As a result of booming AI chip-related revenues and gross profit margin expansion, TSMC generated record profits of T$505.7 billion in Q4 '25 which beat the consensus estimate of T$478.0 billion by a wide margin. TSMC's net income increased 35% year-over-year, meaning the fab's profit growth is about 10 PP higher than its revenue growth in USD terms.

The outlook for 2026 is also very positive and investors will likely see somewhere around 30% top line growth on a full-year basis if TSMC can maintain its current growth momentum, which I believe is likely. Gartner’s projections for 2026 indicate that the Data Center market will continue to expand at double-digits, mainly due to growing CapEx spending on AI-capable chips and other server infrastructure.

With Data Center spending to edge higher in 2026, TSMC's high-performance computing segment is primed for out-performance. HPC continues to dominate TSMC's revenue mix, with the segment representing more than half (55%) of all revenues in the fourth-quarter. Importantly, since Gartner's projections imply double-digit growth (19% Y/Y) in the Data Center system market in 2026, there is a good chance that the demand situation will work in favor of TSMC.

TSMC’s valuation

TSMC has a number of strategic benefits that could translate into a higher earnings-based valuation multiplier in 2026. Most importantly, TSMC's outlook for Q1 '26 highlights strong top line growth expectations as well: the firm's guidance for the first-quarter of FY 2026 calls for revenues between $34.6B and $35.8B which implies a year-over-year growth rate of 38%.

What is most compelling about TSMC is that the fab has an uncontested position in the foundry industry with a ~71% market share (according to Q3 '25 Trendforce figures, the most up-to-date). No other fab is as dominant as TSMC and the second-strongest foundry play, Samsung, only represented a market share of 6.8%, followed by SMIC with a share of 5.1%.

Shares of TSMC are currently trading at a 19.9X P/E ratio, based off of FY 2027 expected earnings, which means they are still some of the cheapest in the industry group. Nvidia, AMD and ASML (ASML) are trading at forward P/E ratios of 24.1X, 21.8X and 32.6X and I have strong buy ratings on all of these companies given sustained growth tailwinds and soaring adoption of AI chips in U.S. Data Centers. Importantly, since TSMC reports its Q4 earnings way earlier than other chip players, the strong earnings scorecard bodes well Nvidia and AMD as well.

In my opinion, shares of TSMC could revalue to a ~25X price-to-earnings ratio, which would be more in-line with the industry group average of 24.5X. TSMC's strong market position, dominance in the fab sector and increasing reliance on the HPC segment in a rapidly growing total addressable market, suggests a strategic advantage that I feel is not yet fully priced into the company's valuation. A recognition of TSMC's potential could lead to a major revaluation of the firm's shares in 2026 as well as new all-time highs. A 25X forward P/E ratio implies a fair value for TSMC in the neighborhood of $412/share which calculates to 26% upside revaluation potential.

TSMC’s risk profile

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is a leading global fab with operations in Southeast Asia as well as a growing manufacturing footprint in the United States. The company has gone on an aggressive expansion spree in the U.S. by boosting its chip manufacturing capabilities. Therefore, TSMC is most vulnerable to a rollback of AI spending plans on the part of hyperscalers. Further, supply and demand dynamics for semiconductors have been historically volatile so changes in demand could quickly trigger an adjustment in order volumes and lead to a drop-off in gross margins.

Closing thoughts

As TSMC’s revenue report earlier this month already indicated, the world’s leading fab is crushing it due to the ongoing AI splurge: TSMC is the ultimate beneficiary of this AI boom in the Data Center market, given its dominant foundry market position, and the chip enterprise should be able to remain highly profitable in 2026, especially with demand for AI-optimized chips set growing.

This year TSMC is likely to grow ~30% on a full-year basis, highlighting an attractive risk/reward relationship for investors since this growth seems underpriced to me, relative to other chip plays available. Maybe most importantly, TSMC is growing its gross profit margins and is becoming increasingly reliant on the high-margin HPC segment... which bodes well for earnings growth this year. TSMC is cheap with a P/E ratio below 20.0X and with shares undervalued by ~21%, I see a favorable investment setup for 2026 as well.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brookfield: A Future $100 Stock Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
Brookfield Corporation (BN)(BN:CA) is a company that had my attention ever since I found out a while ago that Bill Ackman's Pershing Square Holdings (PSHZF) owns it, since I tend to like his stock picks. If you value Brookfield like a traditional finance company -- by using its P/E ratio or P/B ratio, for example -- the stock may look expensive to you. But when you look more under the hood, you realize this stock is actually underpriced.

Brookfield has many moving parts, such as its asset management business, its insurance business, and others. Brookfield will also benefit from growing AI infrastructure demand via its investments in AI data centers, power, and digital networks. It shouldn't be valued like a bank or traditional insurance company or just an asset manager. It's a complicated company that gave me a headache when trying to understand it.

Brookfield should be valued based on its distributable earnings, which have been growing nicely over the years and should continue growing at a 20%+ rate until 2030, according to management. We can also use management's sum-of-the-parts valuation, which values the company at $68 per share compared to its current price of $47. The company has also put its money where its mouth is, buying back shares because they're underpriced, although not a huge amount of shares, admittedly.

When you combine its impressive growth rate, sum of the parts valuation, and the modest forward distributable earnings multiple, the stock looks undervalued. Not only that, but this is a compounder with a strong track record of great performance (1,193% total return in the past 20 years). Therefore, I give BN stock a buy rating.

What Brookfield Corporation Actually Does

Brookfield Corporation fits into the asset management industry, but let's talk a bit more about what you get when you buy Brookfield.

First, according to BN's website, Brookfield has a 73% ownership interest in Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) (BAM:CA), which has over $1 trillion of assets under management and an $86 billion market cap. BAM earns fees for the money it manages.

Second, BN also owns a 26% interest in Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP), which owns and operates essential infrastructure and has a $16 billion market cap. Next, it owns a 46% interest in Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP), which has an $8.7 billion market cap; a 68% stake in Brookfield Business Partners (BBU), with a $3.1 billion market cap; and a 100% stake in Brookfield Property Group, as shown below.

Further, Brookfield owns and controls an insurance and retirement business called Brookfield Wealth Solutions (BNT). This company has a $14.7 billion market cap. BNT sells annuities and insurance policies, meaning it gets premiums upfront that won't have to be paid out for a while, giving BNT money to invest. Brookfield then invests that money into things it's familiar with, like renewables and infrastructure.

Here's what Bruce Flatt, the company's CEO, said specifically about Brookfield Wealth Solutions' strategy, during the Goldman Sachs 2025 U.S. Financial Services Conference:

So our strategy so far has been as opposed to running an insurance company which just invests into traditional -- in traditional ways into both liquid and private credit, our strategy has largely been a barbell approach, not unlike what Berkshire Hathaway has always done, which is hold very large amounts of cash, which we hold today. And that allows you to put significant money into equity strategies, which earn much higher returns.

So don't try to earn 7%, earn 3% in [ 14. ] And if you can earn 3% in [ 14, ] that averages to 7%. But in our view, you can, a, earn a higher return on average if you do your job right, but b, you can take less risk because of the things that we know that we're doing. And the advantage that we have is that the strategies that are the underpinning of Brookfield Asset Management are long-term yielding cash-flow-type assets in renewables, infrastructure, real estate that are very well treated and earn excellent low-risk returns in an insurance company.

Brookfield's various businesses give it access to lots of capital, and according to Bruce Flatt, "Having access to capital wins."

Brookfield's Distributable Earnings: The Figure That Matters

As stated in the intro, book value or EPS shouldn't be used to value Brookfield. You can use distributable earnings instead, which better represents the cash flow available to shareholders and isn't swayed by paper profits.

The company defines distributable earnings as "the sum of distributable earnings from our asset management business, distributable operating earnings from our wealth solutions business, distributions received from our ownership of investments, realized carried interest and disposition gains from principal investments, net of earnings from our Corporate Activities, preferred share dividends, and equity-based compensation costs."

There are also distributable earnings before realizations and capital allocation, which represent core earnings before selling assets at a profit or reinvesting capital into other businesses.

In the Q3-2025 earnings call, Brookfield mentioned that its TTM distributable earnings were $5.4 billion, or $2.27 per share, an 18% increase year-over-year. That's quite the impressive growth rate for an already-large company. That also translates to a distributable earnings multiple of 20.8x, telling a completely different story than the P/E ratio or even the price/book multiple.

If you check its GAAP P/E ratio, you'll see a figure of around 138x, which looks overvalued. Full stop. The price/book of 2.5x also looks pricey when you consider that the book value per share has been flat over the past few years while the stock has gone up.

That's why distributable earnings matter here. The sum of the parts valuation is important too, which I'll get to later.

The AI Opportunity

AI infrastructure is a big opportunity for Brookfield. As Bruce Flatt said during the 2025 Investor Day, it's a $7 trillion opportunity that can possibly be the largest business in the company within 10 years.

On AI, you've seen our slides. This is a $7 trillion opportunity. We're ideally suited because of our power, our adjacencies, our compute infrastructure, our real estate businesses to participate in this. We have a number of AI factories that we're working on, which is around a $200 billion investment project over the next while, and this is just the beginning, and it's really just the start of what's going on in the world on the infrastructure side. This is a multi-decade opportunity that possibly is the largest business within our company within 10 years.

Brookfield is building AI factories, and AI-related companies will pay Brookfield to use these factories. It doesn't matter which AI company wins. Brookfield will collect money either way. AI requires power and real estate, and luckily for Brookfield, it owns both.

The Stock Is Undervalued

At its 2025 Investor Day, Brookfield laid out a sum-of-the-parts valuation that pegs its fair value at $68, with the bulk of the value coming from asset management. At the current price of $47.29, the stock would have to increase by 43.8% to reach $68/share. It makes sense that as of Q3, BN bought back $950 million worth of its shares since management believes the company is undervalued. However, it's worth noting that even annualized, that's a buyback yield of just over 1%, which isn't that high.

Importantly, this plan value per share figure has been increasing. It grew at a 16% CAGR from 2020 to 2025, going from $32 to $68, exceeding management's expectations. Moreover, distributable earnings before realizations grew at an impressive 22% CAGR. Per the Investor Day presentation, the company actually expects a plan value of $140 per share by 2030, implying plenty of upside potential.

The valuation also looks interesting when considering management's forward estimates. For 2030, management has the following projections: $4.60 for DE per share before realizations and capital allocation (17% CAGR), and $6.95 per share for regular distributable earnings (25% CAGR).

That suggests a 10.3x forward multiple for 2030 if you use DE before realizations and capital allocation, or a 6.8x forward multiple for normal DE. Even if BN only ends up with a 15x multiple on its DE by 2030, we're talking about a $104.25 share price, or a 120% gain in five years. Of course, the future DE multiple can be higher than 15x, as it's currently over 20x.

It's not always a great thing to put too much trust in management projections, but given BN's strong track record, I don't see a major reason to doubt them.

Risks: What Could Go Wrong With My Thesis

What can go wrong with my thesis? First, companies like BN that own many other companies are often complicated, misunderstood, or hard to analyze. This can lead to a perpetual conglomerate discount, where the stock always trades at a discount to the sum of its parts because investors often prefer easier-to-analyze companies. So, the discount to the plan value per share is a good starting point, but it's not a guarantee that the gap between the share price and the plan value will close.

In addition, since the company is complex, some investors may not fully understand it, which can lead to panic selling in rough market conditions.

Another risk is relying on management's estimates for distributable earnings since they could be wrong. But as I stated above, I'm comfortable with this risk due to the company's track record.

Lastly, there's always the risk that future AI demand could be overestimated. Industry growth is truly hard to forecast. If data center growth slows, Brookfield's infrastructure investments could take longer to pay off.

The Bottom Line On BN Stock

BN stock is undervalued both when looking at a sum of its parts valuation and its forward distributable earnings multiple. I believe the stock can definitely be worth over $100/share in 5 years if management's distributable earnings estimates are met. Given the company's track record and the large AI opportunity at hand, I'm confident that can happen. What makes its track record more notable is that it was achieved across multiple market cycles and crises, cementing its status as a reliable long-term compounder worth owning.

data.title: Rexford Industrial Realty: A Growing 4% Yield For Lifelong Income
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Whenever I travel, I frequently see new and old storage units all over.

A storage unit is essentially just a garage that's not attached to your home that you can throw items in that you don't need daily, and you refuse to get rid of. I can remember when I moved from one house to another, I got a storage unit that I thought I would have for just a couple of months. Three years later, I finally emptied that storage unit and stopped paying my rent for it. The owner of that storage facility commended me on closing that out and finalizing it because, as he put it, "basically no one does that." They pay the bill for the storage unit and forget about what's in there, never taking time to empty it.

Moving from the personal space to the commercial space, having facilities to store goods before they're sold and after they're manufactured is essential. Many companies love to produce only what they need, but this is not a reality that can persist. If you have an uptick in sales, you don't want to have a waitlist that can deter individuals from buying from your company again in the future. Amazon (AMZN) famously warehouses millions of products so they can deliver them with exceptional speed.

Having to own the buildings you keep your goods in can be an extremely high expense and a logistical nightmare for many companies. This is where real estate investment trusts, REITs, can step in and provide ownership of the buildings and rent them to companies.

Today, I want to look at a way to get paid and have a growing paycheck from a company that warehouses goods for others.

Let's dive in!

Get Paid For Storing Goods

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) acquires, manages, and redevelops prime infill industrial assets throughout Southern California. As of Sept. 30, 2025, the company owned roughly 51 million square feet of industrial real estate.

Without properties such as REXR's, the United States economy would be in a world of hurt. This is because an estimated 30% of total U.S. port volume is processed at the Ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach. By itself, Southern California is the planet's fourth-largest industrial market, only ranked behind the U.S., China, and Japan.

Combined with the constrained supply of industrial real estate in its markets, this affords the REIT a great deal of pricing power. REXR's average annual lease rate as of Q3 2025 was just above $17 per square foot. Through the first nine months of 2025, the industrial REIT has leased 7.4 million square feet with an average embedded rent step of 3.6%. That provides an organic means of growth for REXR.

The industrial REIT can also lean on redevelopment activity to drive growth in the years ahead.
